text: Well, I've got to admit, this one came
out of nowhere. I can't really explain
how, but for some reason, I knew next to
nothing about this movie or the guy who
made it or the lore behind it until like
a couple of days ago when suddenly
everyone was talking about Iron Lung. I
mean, can you really blame me, though? A
YouTuber making a low-budget independent
horror movie? Haven't we just done that?
Well, yeah, but the difference here is
that Iron Lung is actually good. Taking
a wildly outlandish sci-fi concept and
wrapping it up into a tense,
claustrophobic story of human
perseverance and cosmic Lovecraftian
horror driven by a compelling script
that keeps you guessing about where it's
all leading and a surprisingly strong
performance from its lead actor. In
short, this is exactly what independent
filmm should be about. Yeah, it's
definitely not perfect, and if I'm
honest, it feels more like a short film
concept stretched out into a 2-hour
runtime, but I'll get into that later.
For now, let us discuss the plot, shall
we? So, backstory for Iron Long is a bit
thin on the ground, but as far as I can
tell, the film set at some indeterminate
point in the future where humanity's
begun to colonize the galaxy until an
event known as the Quiet Rapture caused
all the stars and planets to disappear.
How? Don't know. Just go with it. The
only survivors now were scattered across
the void on ships and space stations,
including our main man, Simon, a
convicted prisoner who was involved in
the destruction of an outpost called
Filament Station. What exactly happened?
Don't know. His mission is to pilot a
submersible into an ocean of blood on a
distant moon and gather samples from the
seabed where his commanders seem to
think they'll find information that
could save the human race. Wait, why is
there an entire ocean of blood? Why do
they think there's stuff down there
that's going to save humanity? Why use a
convicted felon with zero training for
such an important mission? Don't know.
Just go with it. Anyway, so down he goes
into the deep
ocean.
And because you can't really see through
blood, he's forced to use an X-ray
camera to take quick snapshots of his
surroundings. That's all you ever get to
see of what's around him. And it's a
neat little way of circumventing the
film's lack of budget. Anyway, that's
when he spots a skeleton lying on the
ocean floor. And for some reason, this
is super exciting news for everyone
involved. I mean, I'm guessing if you're
in a literal ocean of blood, it probably
means that quite a few things had to die
to create it. So, there would definitely
be skeletons involved, but whatever. His
commander wants him to gather more data,
but then he gets attacked by an undersea
monster that sever communications and
leaves him stranded in some underwater
cave system. From there on, he has to
figure out a way to escape the labyrinth
in a poorly maintained submarine whose
systems are starting to fail while
suffering increasingly severe
hallucinations and intermittent radio
contact with a survivor from a previous
expedition. But can he hope to ever make
it back to the surface? What's that
undersea monster all about? Why is the
blood leaking into his submarine? And
what is it doing to his body? And is the
voice on the radio even real? You know,
it's interesting to compare this movie
to Shelby Oaks because on the surface,
the two films have a lot in common.
They're both independent horror movies
made by YouTube personalities with
roughly comparable budgets. But the
difference is the creative philosophy
behind them. Whereas Shelby Oaks was
basically a patchwork quilt of tried and
tested horror tropes wrapped up in a
safe, conventional plot that's been done
a million times before, Iron Lung is a
risky, wildly inventive concept, setting
up an interesting and creative premise
that allows it to make the most of
limited resources and constantly keeping
you guessing about where it's all
heading. One is an actual creative
horror movie willing to take risks made
by people with a story to tell. The
other feels more like an AI's idea of
what a horror movie should be, made out
of obligation rather than artistic
passion. And well, the results speak for
themselves. One practically vanished
without a trace, and the other seems to
be making waves at the box office. Not
to worry, though, Chris. I'm sure
there's plenty more predatory gambling
apps for you to shill. He's out of line,
but he's right.
>> The other big surprise for me was
Markiplier as lead actor. Well,
basically the only actor now that I
think about it. I mean, let's be real
here. Financing your own movie so that
you yourself can star in it is a risky
move that could have turned the whole
thing into a laughable vanity project.
YouTubers aren't exactly known for their
dramatic acting range, especially if
they have to carry an entire movie by
themselves. But credit to the guy, he
pulls off a surprisingly strong,
compelling performance as a desperate
man pushed to his mental and physical
limits with the vague promise of freedom
at the ends. It's all good stuff, and
you can't help but root for the guy. I
said before that Iron Lung's only real
weakness is the sluggish pace, mainly
caused by the lack of real plot events
and other characters to bounce off. The
film's 2 hours long, and although it
definitely gets its momentum back for
the finale, the second act is pretty
slow and tedious. I'm not going to lie,
I was starting to lose focus on what the
protagonist was actually doing and
getting a little bored of being trapped
in one location for the entire film. I
get that it's supposed to play into the
film's atmosphere of claustrophobia and
paranoia, and it's obviously a direct
copy of the video game that it's based
on, but there's an obvious distinction
between tension and boredom when it
stretched too far, and Iron Long
definitely crosses the line at times. I
can't shake the feeling that this setup
would have made for an awesome 30inut
short film rather than a drawn out
2-hour feature, but what the [ __ ]
Nobody's going to a movie theater to see
a 30inute film. Overall though, I think
Iron Long is a movie that's way better
than it has any right to be. It's well
shot, well acted, and although it
doesn't pretend to answer all the
questions it poses, I'm perfectly okay
with that. Cosmic horror is a genre
that's been chronically underrepresented
in cinema, so it's nice to see a film
tackling it in a unique, inventive way
like this. It might not be perfect, but
I never needed it to be. I just needed
it to be good, and it is. So, well done,
Markiplier. You took a gamble and it
paid off. Oh, wait. Maybe I shouldn't
mention gambling.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
